docs.title: ABMForge Documentation
docs.text:
ABMForge is a lightweight, reproducible, experiment-native agent-based modeling framework for Python.

It is designed for researchers, educators, model developers, data scientists, and students who want to build agent-based simulations that are reproducible, analyzable, and extensible.

docs.title: Core Principles
docs.text: ABMForge is built around four principles:

docs.title: Reproducibility
docs.text: Simulation runs should be repeatable through explicit seeds, run metadata, manifests, and snapshots.

docs.title: Experiment-Native Design
docs.text: Experiments are first-class concepts rather than afterthoughts.

docs.title: Dataset-First Outputs
docs.text: Simulation outputs should be easy to analyze using standard data tools.

docs.title: Lightweight Python Architecture
docs.text: The core framework should remain easy to understand, easy to test, and easy to extend.

docs.title: Analysis
docs.text: ABMForge includes basic analysis helpers for experiment outputs.

docs.title: SensitivityAnalysis
docs.text:
SensitivityAnalysis estimates simple parameter effects using final model-level metric values.

from abmforge import SensitivityAnalysis

analysis = SensitivityAnalysis(
    experiment_result,
    metric="total_wealth",
)

summary = analysis.summary()
print(summary)

docs.title: SALib Integration
docs.text:
SALib support is optional.

Install analysis dependencies:

pip install -e ".[analysis]"

docs.title: Sobol Sampling
docs.text:
from abmforge import SALibProblem, sample_sobol

problem = SALibProblem(
    bounds={
        "density": (0.4, 0.9),
        "homophily": (0.1, 0.8),
    }
)

samples = sample_sobol(problem, n=128, seed=42)

docs.title: Morris Sampling
docs.text:
from abmforge import SALibProblem, sample_morris

problem = SALibProblem(
    bounds={
        "density": (0.4, 0.9),
        "homophily": (0.1, 0.8),
    }
)

samples = sample_morris(problem, n=32, seed=42)

docs.title: Recommended Workflow
docs.text:
1. Define parameter ranges.
  2. Generate samples.
  3. Run model scenarios.
  4. Collect final metrics.
  5. Analyze sensitivity.

docs.title: API Stability
docs.text:
ABMForge is currently alpha-stage software. Public APIs may still evolve before v1.0.0.

This document defines the intended stability level of the main public APIs.

docs.title: Stable Candidate
docs.text:
APIs in this category are expected to remain mostly stable until v1.0.0.

Minor additions are allowed, but breaking changes should be avoided unless strongly justified.

docs.title: Experimental
docs.text: APIs in this category may change as the framework evolves.

docs.title: Internal
docs.text: APIs in this category are not intended for direct user use.

docs.title: Experimental APIs
docs.text:
The following areas are considered experimental:

  • full replay and model reconstruction
  • plugin architecture
  • advanced storage backends
  • high-performance columnar agent backends
  • advanced calibration interfaces
  • GIS integrations beyond point coordinates

docs.title: Internal APIs
docs.text: Anything not exported from abmforge.__init__ should be considered internal unless documented otherwise.

docs.title: Breaking Change Policy
docs.text:
Before v1.0.0, breaking changes may occur, but they should follow these rules:

  1. Prefer additive changes.
  2. Avoid renaming public classes and methods.
  3. Deprecate before removing when possible.
  4. Document migration steps.
  5. Update examples and tests in the same pull request.

docs.title: v1.0.0 Criteria
docs.text:
ABMForge should reach v1.0.0 only when:

  • the public API is documented
  • core examples are stable
  • CI passes across supported Python versions
  • documentation is published
  • test coverage remains above the target threshold
  • release notes clearly describe supported features

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text: Contributions are welcome.

docs.title: Development Setup
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/fatihuludag-lab/abmforge.git
cd abmforge
pip install -e ".[dev]"

docs.title: Checks
docs.text:
Run before opening a pull request:

ruff format src tests examples
ruff check src tests examples
mypy src
pytest
python3 -m build

docs.title: Good First Contributions
docs.text:
Good first areas include:

  • documentation
  • examples
  • tests
  • schedulers
  • spaces
  • export formats
  • visualization helpers
  • analysis tools

docs.title: Pull Request Expectations
docs.text:
A pull request should include:

  • a clear description
  • tests when changing code
  • documentation when adding public features
  • passing CI checks

docs.title: Experiments
docs.text:
ABMForge treats experiments as first-class objects.

A typical research workflow is:

Model → Scenario → Experiment → Dataset → Analysis

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text:
A Scenario represents one reproducible model run.

from abmforge import Scenario

scenario = Scenario(
    model=MyModel,
    parameters={"alpha": 0.5},
    seed=42,
    steps=100,
)

result = scenario.run()

docs.title: Experiment
docs.text:
An Experiment runs multiple scenarios generated from parameter combinations and seeds.

from abmforge import Experiment

experiment = Experiment(
    model=MyModel,
    parameters={
        "alpha": [0.1, 0.5, 0.9],
        "beta": [1, 2],
    },
    seeds=[1, 2, 3],
    steps=100,
)

result = experiment.run()

This creates:

3 alpha values × 2 beta values × 3 seeds = 18 runs

docs.title: ParameterGrid
docs.text:
from abmforge import ParameterGrid

grid = ParameterGrid(
    {
        "density": [0.6, 0.8],
        "homophily": [0.3, 0.5],
    }
)

for parameters in grid:
    print(parameters)

docs.title: ExperimentResult
docs.text:
summary = result.summary()

Example:

{
    "run_count": 18,
    "successful_count": 18,
    "failed_count": 0,
    "statuses": {"completed": 18},
}

docs.title: Export
docs.text:
result.write_csv("outputs/experiment")

This writes combined files such as:

  • runs.csv
  • model_records.csv
  • agent_records.csv

docs.title: When to Use Experiments
docs.text:
Use Experiment when you need:

  • parameter sweeps
  • repeated random seeds
  • robustness checks
  • sensitivity analysis
  • reproducible computational experiments

docs.title: First Model
docs.text: This page shows how to build a minimal ABMForge model.

docs.title: Basic Structure
docs.text:
An ABMForge model usually has two classes:

  • an Agent subclass
  • a Model subclass

The agent defines individual behavior. The model defines setup, scheduling, recording, and simulation logic.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
from abmforge import Agent, Model, Scenario
from abmforge.scheduling import RandomActivation


class Person(Agent):
    def step(self) -> None:
        self.wealth += 1


class WealthModel(Model):
    def setup(self) -> None:
        self.agents.create(Person, n=100, wealth=0)
        self.scheduler = RandomActivation(self)

        self.record.metric(
            "total_wealth",
            lambda model: model.agents.sum("wealth"),
        )

    def step(self) -> None:
        self.scheduler.step()


scenario = Scenario(
    model=WealthModel,
    seed=42,
    steps=10,
)

result = scenario.run()

print(result.dataset.model_records)

docs.title: What Happens?
docs.text:
1. Person defines agent-level behavior.
  2. WealthModel.setup() creates agents.
  3. RandomActivation activates agents in random order.
  4. record.metric() records model-level data.
  5. Scenario runs the model reproducibly.
  6. result.dataset stores output records.

docs.title: Agent
docs.text:
Agents are Python objects with access to:

  • self.model
  • self.unique_id
  • self.rng

docs.title: Model
docs.text:
Models define:

  • parameters
  • random generator
  • agents
  • event queue
  • recorder
  • world or space

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text: A scenario describes one reproducible model run.

docs.title: Dataset
docs.text:
The dataset stores:

  • run metadata
  • model-level records
  • agent-level records
  • event records
  • lifecycle records

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
Clone the repository:

git clone https://github.com/fatihuludag-lab/abmforge.git
cd abmforge

Install development dependencies:

pip install -e ".[dev]"

Optional extras:

pip install -e ".[viz]"
pip install -e ".[analysis]"
pip install -e ".[docs]"
pip install -e ".[all]"

docs.title: Run the Test Suite
docs.text:
ruff format src tests examples
ruff check src tests examples
mypy src
pytest
python3 -m build

docs.title: First Scenario
docs.text:
from abmforge import Scenario

scenario = Scenario(
    model=MyModel,
    seed=42,
    steps=100,
)

result = scenario.run()

docs.title: Dataset Export
docs.text:
result.dataset.write_csv("outputs/run")
result.dataset.write_json("outputs/run")
result.dataset.write_manifest("outputs/run")

docs.title: Experiment
docs.text:
from abmforge import Experiment

experiment = Experiment(
    model=MyModel,
    parameters={
        "alpha": [0.1, 0.5],
        "beta": [1, 2],
    },
    seeds=[1, 2, 3],
    steps=100,
)

result = experiment.run()
print(result.summary())
result.write_csv("outputs/experiment")

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Run bundled examples from the repository root:

python3 examples/wealth_model/run.py
python3 examples/schelling/run.py
python3 examples/sir_epidemic/run.py
python3 examples/sugarscape/run.py
python3 examples/parameter_sweep/run.py
python3 examples/gis_space/run.py

docs.title: GISSpace
docs.text:
GISSpace is a lightweight geographic space for longitude-latitude coordinates.

It is designed for:

  • urban simulation
  • transportation models
  • mobility studies
  • spatial epidemiology
  • geographic agent placement

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
from abmforge import Agent, GISSpace, Model


class CityAgent(Agent):
    pass


model = Model(seed=42)
ankara = model.agents.create(CityAgent, n=1)[0]
istanbul = model.agents.create(CityAgent, n=1)[0]

space = GISSpace()
space.place(ankara, (32.8597, 39.9334))
space.place(istanbul, (28.9784, 41.0082))

distance = space.distance(ankara, istanbul)

docs.title: GeoJSON Export
docs.text: geojson = space.to_geojson()

docs.title: Current Scope
docs.text:
GISSpace currently supports:

  • point coordinates
  • haversine distance
  • radius-based neighbor queries
  • GeoJSON point export

Future versions may support:

  • GeoPandas integration
  • shapefile loading
  • polygon containment
  • routing
  • raster data

docs.title: Replay and Snapshots
docs.text: ABMForge provides simple snapshot read/write helpers.

docs.title: Write a Snapshot
docs.text:
from abmforge import write_snapshot

snapshot = model.snapshot()
write_snapshot(snapshot, "outputs/snapshot.json")

docs.title: Read a Snapshot
docs.text:
from abmforge import read_snapshot

snapshot = read_snapshot("outputs/snapshot.json")

docs.title: Current Scope
docs.text:
The current snapshot system stores JSON-serializable model state.

Future versions may support:

  • full replay
  • event trace replay
  • deterministic debugging
  • snapshot comparison
  • checkpointing

docs.title: Scheduling
docs.text:
Scheduling controls the order in which agents act.

ABMForge provides several activation strategies.

docs.title: SequentialActivation
docs.text:
Activates agents in insertion order.

from abmforge.scheduling import SequentialActivation

self.scheduler = SequentialActivation(self)

docs.title: RandomActivation
docs.text:
Activates agents in a deterministic random order using the model-level RNG.

from abmforge.scheduling import RandomActivation

self.scheduler = RandomActivation(self)

docs.title: SimultaneousActivation
docs.text:
Calls step() for all agents, then calls advance() for agents that define it.

from abmforge.scheduling import SimultaneousActivation

self.scheduler = SimultaneousActivation(self)

docs.title: StagedActivation
docs.text:
Calls named methods on agents in a specified order.

from abmforge.scheduling import StagedActivation

self.scheduler = StagedActivation(
    self,
    stages=["sense", "decide", "act"],
    shuffle=True,
)

docs.title: Choosing a Scheduler
docs.text: Scheduler Use when SequentialActivation deterministic ordering is desired RandomActivation random activation is part of the model assumption SimultaneousActivation agents should update together StagedActivation behavior has explicit phases

docs.title: Spaces
docs.text: ABMForge supports several environment types for agent interaction.

docs.title: GridWorld
docs.text:
GridWorld is a two-dimensional discrete grid.

It is useful for:

  • segregation models
  • epidemic models
  • cellular models
  • resource landscapes
from abmforge import GridWorld

world = GridWorld(
    width=20,
    height=20,
    torus=True,
    multi=False,
)

world.place(agent, (5, 5))
world.move(agent, (6, 5))

neighbors = world.neighbors(
    agent,
    radius=1,
    include_center=False,
)

docs.title: NetworkSpace
docs.text:
NetworkSpace supports graph-based agent interaction.

It is useful for:

  • social networks
  • contagion models
  • opinion dynamics
  • information diffusion
from abmforge import NetworkSpace

space = NetworkSpace()
space.add_edge("a", "b")
space.place(agent, "a")

docs.title: ContinuousSpace
docs.text:
ContinuousSpace supports continuous two-dimensional coordinates.

It is useful for:

  • mobility models
  • swarm models
  • ecology models
  • evacuation models
from abmforge import ContinuousSpace

space = ContinuousSpace(
    width=100.0,
    height=100.0,
    torus=True,
)

docs.title: GISSpace
docs.text:
GISSpace supports longitude-latitude coordinates and distance queries.

It is useful for:

  • urban simulation
  • mobility studies
  • spatial epidemiology
  • transportation models
from abmforge import GISSpace

space = GISSpace()
space.place(agent, (32.8597, 39.9334))

distance_km = space.distance(agent_a, agent_b)
geojson = space.to_geojson()

docs.title: Choosing a Space
docs.text: Space Best for GridWorld discrete spatial ABMs NetworkSpace relational and network ABMs ContinuousSpace continuous movement GISSpace geographic coordinate models

docs.title: Visualization
docs.text:
Visualization helpers are optional and require matplotlib.

Install visualization dependencies:

pip install -e ".[viz]"

docs.title: Time Series
docs.text:
from abmforge import plot_timeseries

plot_timeseries(
    result.dataset,
    metric="infected",
)

docs.title: Multiple Runs
docs.text:
from abmforge import plot_multiple_runs

plot_multiple_runs(
    experiment_result,
    metric="mean_wealth",
)

docs.title: GridWorld
docs.text:
from abmforge import plot_grid

plot_grid(model.world)

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: Visualization helpers are intentionally lightweight. They are designed for quick inspection and teaching workflows rather than publication-ready figures.
